critic_raw: The escalation prediction experiment is generally well-designed. Let me challenge the temporal split strategy and class imbalance handling more deeply. The design uses 24 months for training, 6 for validation, 6 for test. However, 24 months of training data for a SaaS company means the model may have learned to recognize escalation patterns associated with older product versions that are no longer relevant in months 31-36. The temporal drift could make the large training window a liability. Additionally, the class imbalance handling uses 'inverse class frequency weighting' — but for PR-AUC evaluation, the choice of class weights at training time affects the precision-recall tradeoff. Different weighting schemes could shift the PR curve significantly without changing the fundamental model quality.
defender_raw: Round 1: On temporal drift from long training window: temporal drift is explicitly tested via the temporal design. If the model learns product-version-specific patterns that become obsolete, test PR-AUC will be lower than validation PR-AUC, signaling this. Moreover, the 24-month training window is designed to provide sufficient training signal — shorter training data would produce worse models with higher variance. The product-line stratification would reveal product-specific degradation if old-version patterns dominate. On class weighting and PR-AUC: inverse class frequency weighting is applied consistently to both models and both are evaluated on the same test data. Class weights affect the precision-recall operating point during training but the PR-AUC metric integrates across all thresholds — class weighting choice at training time does not fundamentally affect the PR-AUC comparison since both models use the same weighting scheme.

rounds.critic_followup: On class weighting and PR-AUC: you say both models use the same weighting. But inverse class frequency weighting makes the loss function treat minority-class (escalation) samples as more important. For a neural network (transformer), this changes the gradient landscape fundamentally. For logistic regression, it primarily shifts the decision boundary. Does this mean the models are optimized for different operating points on the PR curve, making the PR-AUC comparison less straightforward?
rounds.defender_followup: PR-AUC is threshold-agnostic — it integrates area under the precision-recall curve across all possible classification thresholds. Class weighting affects how the model calibrates its scores, but PR-AUC compares the full precision-recall curve shapes, not a single operating point. A model that is well-calibrated across thresholds will have higher PR-AUC regardless of training class weights. The Expected Calibration Error secondary metric explicitly tests whether predicted probabilities align with true escalation rates — this directly checks for the calibration effects of class weighting. The design is comprehensive in this regard.

rounds.critic_followup: On the long training window: you note that product-line stratification would reveal old-version pattern dominance. But what if the model's 24-month training captures genuine long-term escalation patterns (not just version-specific ones) — how do we distinguish improvement from temporal data volume vs. improvement from the transformer's architectural advantages?
rounds.defender_followup: Both models train on the same 24-month window, so the temporal data volume is identical for both. Any advantage from more data accrues equally to transformer and logistic regression. The comparison isolates architecture (sequence modeling with timing) from feature engineering (BoW vs. full sequence). If both models benefit equally from 24 months of data, the relative advantage is still architectural. If the transformer gains disproportionately from temporal volume (e.g., it better leverages diverse escalation patterns over time), that is an intrinsic architectural strength, not a confound. The design correctly attributes differences to architecture given equal data access.

adjudication_raw: Two concerns probed over two rounds: temporal drift from long training window, and class weighting effects on PR-AUC comparison. Both resolved: temporal drift is self-diagnosing via train/val/test PR-AUC comparison; PR-AUC is threshold-agnostic and ECE checks calibration; both models share equal data access. Verdict: defense_wins.
